coredata.dc:title: Nanozymatic detection of thiocyanate through accelerating the growth of ultra-small gold nanoparticles/graphene quantum dots hybrids
coredata.dc:creator: Rahin Ahmed, Syed
Sherazee, Masoomeh
Srinivasan, Seshasai
Reza Rajabzadeh, Amin
coredata.dc:description: Thiocyanate (SCN−) concentration monitoring in food is important to ensure the health and safety of the consumers.A colorimetric detection of thiocyanate (SCN−) based on the nanozymatic activity of gold nanoparticle-graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Au NPs) hybrids in the presence of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and H2O2 has been proposed. Here, a new synthesis method of GQDs directly from graphite was introduced. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images revealed that the size of the GQDs was 3–5 nm, and the emission peak appeared at 450 nm. As-synthesized GQDs was utilized to produce GQDs-Au NPs hybrids without additional chemicals. However, the presence of SCN− inhibits the growth of Au NPs, the resulting Au NPs are smaller in size. Moreover, SCN− group is well-known for hydroxyl radical (OH) scavenging activity that could oxidize TMB. Both effects boosted the nanozymatic activity of GQDs-Au NPs to detect SCN− under optimized conditions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3 nM. Present study also validates the methodology to detect SCN− in raw milk.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thiocyanate detection
GQDs-Au NPs hybrids
Nanozyme
Colorimetric assay

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Au NPs using GQDs
Spectroscopic and microscopic studies of GQDs
Spectroscopic and microscopic studies of Au NPs
Spectroscopic and microscopic studies of GQDs-Au NPs hybrids
Spectroscopic and microscopic studies of GQDs-Au NPs hybrids in the presence of SCN−

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of proposed sensing mechanism.

Synthesis and characterization of GQDs: (A) Schematic presentation of GQDs synthesis; (B) absorbance of GQDs; (C) excitation and emission wavelength of GQDs; (D) HRTEM image of GQDs (inset: color of GQDs under UV light irradiation and lattice fringe pattern of GQDs) and (E) Raman spectra of GQDs.

Characterization of Au NPs and GQDs-Au NPs hybrids: (A) TEM image of Au NPs; (B) absorbance of Au NPs; (C) SAED image of Au NPs; (D) TEM image of GQDs-Au NPs hybrids; (E) absorbance of GQDs-Au NPs hybrids; (F) SAED image of GQDs-Au NPs hybrids; (G) TEM image of GQDs-Au NPs hybrids in the presence of SCN−; (H) absorbance of GQDs-Au NPs hybrids in the presence of SCN−; (I) SAED image of GQDs-Au NPs hybrids in the presence of SCN−.

Nanozymatic detection of SCN−: (A) absorbance peak of blue-colored solution in the presence of different concentrations of SCN−; (B) the calibration curve of absorbance at 650 vs. the concentration of SCN−. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The selectivity of the SCN− assay and the detection of SCN− in milk: (A) selectivity of the SCN− detection (a: SCN− b: BSA, c: fructose, d: sucrose, e: citrate, f: lysine, g: Mg2+, h: Ca2+, i: EDTA, j: SO4 2−, k: CO3 2−, l: Cl−, m: CH3CO−, n: NO3 −; o: cysteine, p: glutathione, (B) the calibration curve of absorbance at 650 vs. the concentration of SCN− in spiked milk samples.
Comparison study of the present method with the others reported SCN− detection.

head.ce:title: Nanozymatic detection of thiocyanate through accelerating the growth of ultra-small gold nanoparticles/graphene quantum dots hybrids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering Practice and Technology, McMaster University, 1280 Main Street West Hamilton, Ontario L8S 4L7, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Engineering Practice and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering Practice and Technology, McMaster University, 1280 Main Street West Hamilton, Ontario L8S 4L7, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thiocyanate (SCN−) concentration monitoring in food is important to ensure the health and safety of the consumers.A colorimetric detection of thiocyanate (SCN−) based on the nanozymatic activity of gold nanoparticle-graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Au NPs) hybrids in the presence of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and H2O2 has been proposed. Here, a new synthesis method of GQDs directly from graphite was introduced. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images revealed that the size of the GQDs was 3–5 nm, and the emission peak appeared at 450 nm. As-synthesized GQDs was utilized to produce GQDs-Au NPs hybrids without additional chemicals. However, the presence of SCN− inhibits the growth of Au NPs, the resulting Au NPs are smaller in size. Moreover, SCN− group is well-known for hydroxyl radical (OH) scavenging activity that could oxidize TMB. Both effects boosted the nanozymatic activity of GQDs-Au NPs to detect SCN− under optimized conditions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3 nM. Present study also validates the methodology to detect SCN− in raw milk.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of large concentrations of SCN− in mammals is known to cause iodine deficiency via competitive inhibition of the sodium-iodide symporter (NIS) (Laurberg et al., 2009). SCN− also promotes iodide efflux from the thyroid gland, further diminishing adequate iodide in the body. This can lead to more profound health complications such as developing goiters, experiencing vertigo, nasal bleeding, and even, in extreme cases, unconsciousness (Peng, Pan, Zhi-Juan, Wei, & Shao, 2017; Willemin & Lumen, 2019). SCN− is found in lesser quantities in cow’s milk. However, in dairy industries across the globe, it has become commonplace to add more SCN− due to its bacteriostatic effects resulting in greater longevity of the milk product. Although financially beneficial, this practice could be detrimental as consuming these foods in excess potentiates the incurrence of developing hypothyroidism. Hence, detecting and monitoring the levels of SCN− in food becomes of paramount importance when it comes to ensuring the health and safety of communities around the world.

To date, several methods have been proposed for the detection of SCN- across varying media samples such as saliva, plasma, and urine. These include UV–vis spectrometry, fluorimetry, capillary electrophoresis, ion chromatography, gas chromatography, electrochemistry, Surface Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS), HPLC-fluorimetry and conventional colorimetric method (Hussain, Pu, & Sun, 2020; Akiyama et al., 2006; Song, Wu, Wan, & Ma, 2015; Song, Huang, Wan, & Wu, 2016). However, most of those methods are sub-ideal because they are expensive, difficult to transport, require too many materials, and wasteful in terms of time and resources (Pena-Pereira, Lavilla, & Bendicho, 2016). Therefore, developing a SCN- detection method that is simple, rapid, reliable, and sensitive is urgently needed.

The advancement of nanotechnology in food detection has gained huge research interest because of its rapidity, convenience, greater selectivity, and cost-effectiveness (Omanović-Mikličanin and Maksimović, 2016). In particular, nanozymes are especially coveted because they are, overall, more efficient, do not demand skilled technicians, and in some cases, results can be concluded with simple eye observation. This is attributed to features such as increased stability, simpler and quicker alteration and adaptation, and being relatively inexpensive when compared to traditional enzymes (Wang & Gunasekaran, 2020). There are several established applications of nanozymes as food sensors. Huang et al. comprehensively discuss in their article such uses; for instance, nanozymes as signal tags or amplifiers, nanozyme-based assays, and other multifunctional sensing features (Huang, Sun, Pu, & Wei, 2019). These mechanisms can then be carried forward to quality control initiatives involving food composition analyses, contaminant detection, and quantification. Adaptations of nanozymes for biosensors are versatile, with potential implementation as colorimetric, fluorescence, chemiluminescent, electrochemical, and SERS-based biosensors among others (Ahmed, Cardoso, Kumar, Ortega, Srinivasan, & Rajabzadeh, 2021a; Ahangari, Kurbanoglu, Ehsani, & Uslu, 2021). Amid different nanomaterials, the nanozymatic nature of Au NPs stand out with several distinctive features because of precise structural control through colloidal syntheses, inertness, outstanding catalytic durability in different working conditions, ease of surface modification, wide range of pH and temperature stability, size, and shape-based tunning of properties (Ahmed, Chand et al., 2020a; Ahmed & Chen, 2020b; Ahmed, Corredor, Nagy, & Neethirajan, 2017; Ahmed, Kim, Suzuki, Lee, & Park, 2016b, 2016a; Ahmed, Kim, et al., 2017; Ahmed, Takemeura, et al., 2017; Oh et al., 2018). In addition, Au NPs exhibit multimodal enzyme-mimicking behaviors and dynamically switching among peroxidase-, catalase-, oxidase-, and superoxide dismutase-imitating modes upon variation of reaction parameters. Considering all those unique advantages, the use of Au NPs in enzyme-mimicking applications has expanding rapidly and exhibiting superior over other inorganic nanomaterials (Sharifi et al., 2020).

To date, several articles have been reported in the biosensing field regarding the nanozymatic activity of Au nanoparticles and nanocomposites. However, the nanozymatic activity of Au NPs based on the surface ligand hasn’t been extensively studied yet. It is a well-known mechanism in the case of peroxidase-like activity of nanomaterials that, at first, H2O2 adsorbs onto the surface of Au NPs and breaks into hydroxyl radicals (OH). TMB oxidizes in the presence of OH radicals and produces a blue color. The intensity of the color is proportionally related to the concentration of the target analyte(Ahmed, Cirone, & Chen, 2019). Hence, the greater the confinement of OH radicals on the surface of Au NPs, the stronger its nanozymatic activity. The present study aims to utilize this strategy to make a more sensitive SCN− detection method and conduce a new era of biosensor. Carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials have also gained enormous research interest in the field of biosensing due to their biocompatibility, tunning emission wavelength and ease of preparation (Sun, Wang, & Lei, 2015).

In particular, GQDs showed promising advancements in several biosensing applications due to their ultra-small size, which enables the attachment of more analytes to its surface and leads to greater biocompatibility, increased photostability, and tunable fluorescence emission properties. Besides those properties, several other intriguing properties, for example, edge effects and quantum confinement effects, added more potentiality of GQDs for biomedical applications (Ahmed, Kumar, Ortega, Srinivasan, & Rajabzadeh, 2021b; Ahmed, Mogus, Chand, Nagy, & Neethirajan, 2018; Zor et al., 2015). In addition, the carboxylic functional groups on the surface of GQDs allow for greater water dispersibility and provide more facilities to conjugate with biological compounds for various applications. Presently, several synthesis methods of GQDs have been reported, for example, hydrothermal method (Xie, Lai, & Huq, 2017), plasma treatment (D. Liu et al., 2018), electrochemical method (Ahirwar, Mallick, & Bahadur, 2017), and microwave-assisted synthesis (Singh, Kumar, Singh, Savu, & Moshkalev, 2019). Most of those synthesis methods used graphene as a precursor material that derivates from graphite. In this study, we have developed a new synthesis method of GQDs directly from graphite combining chemical treatment and hydrothermal approach. Moreover, GQDs can generate Au NPs from their precursor and stabilize the nanoparticles. This study also reports how the presence of SCN− groups could tune the size of Au NPs and boost its nanozymatic activity. By monitoring the change in the developed blue color, as a consequence of nanozymatic reaction, a colorimetric sensing method of SCN− detection has been proposed.

Gold (III)chloridetrihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), trisodium citrate, graphite nanopowder, sodium thiocyanide, phosphate buffer, potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), H2O2 terephthalic acid, dialysis bag, bovine serum albumin, fructose, sucrose, trisodium citrate, lysine, MgCl2, CaCl2, EDTA, Na2SO4, Na2CO3, NaCl, m: CH3CONa, NaNO3 and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. All analytical grade chemicals were used without further purification.

In this study, a new two-step approach of GQDs synthesis is illustrated. At first, graphite nanopowder (1 mg mL −1) was oxidized by KMnO4 (3 mg) in 10 mL of concentrated H2SO4 at 50 °C for 2 h. Then, 100 mL H2O was slowly added, and the oxidation process continued for 15 min at 95 °C. The resulting solution was purified through centrifugation (15000 rpm, 30 min) several times and dried in an oven. In the second step, 10 mL (1 mg mL−1) ofdried product was mixed with trisodium citrate (1 M, final concentration) and autoclaved for 2 h at 150 °C. At this stage, the carboxylic group will stabilize the yellow-colored GQDs solution, which was cooled down and filtered through a 0.22-μm microporous membrane to remove the unreacted portion. The as-prepared GQDs were further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3,000 to 8,000 Da) and redispersed in MilliQ water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Au NPs using GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs assisted synthesis of Au NPs was prepared as follows: aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (0.05 mM) was mixed with as-synthesized GQDs (0.05 mg mL−1) and gently stirred at the room temperature, RT, for 30 min. The solution color turned purple, indicating the formation of Au NPs. Here, citrate capped-GQDs act as reducing agents and stabilizers of Au NPs.

To obtain the highest peroxidase-like activity from nanohybrids, several parameters, including the concentrations of GQDs, HAuCl4, H2O2, and TMB were optimized. The optimizations were performed at the room temperature for the GQDs concentration range of 0.01 to 0.09 mg mL−1 with an interval of 0.02 mg mL−1, HAuCl4 concentration range of 0.01 to 0.09 mM with an interval of 0.02 mM, H2O2 concentration range from 0 to 18 mM with an interval of 2 mM and TMB concentration range from 1 to 9 mM with an interval of 1 mM. The characteristic blue color developed in those reactions was recorded using UV–vis spectrometer.

A series of different concentrations of SCN− (50 µL) were mixed with GQDs solution (100 µL, 0.05 mg mL−1) separately in a 96-well plate. Then, HAuCl4 solution (0.05 mM, 50 µL) was added to each of the above solutions and gently stirred at RT for 30 min. The formation of Au NPs in different solutions was recorded using UV–vis spectra immediately. Then, a mixture solution (50 µL) of TMB (5 mM) and H2O2 (10 mM) was added to each well separately, and the blue color developed through nanozymatic reaction was recorded using UV–vis spectrometer.

The presence of hydroxyl radicals (OH) in the reaction media was confirmed through the TA test (Ahmed et al., 2019). Here, H2O2 molecules decompose to form the OH radicals, producing a fluorescence signal in the presence of TA. In this study, 0.5 mM (200 μL) aqueous solution of TA was induced to react with citrate-capped GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids and SCN−-capped GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids (50 μL. 4.7 nM) for 10 min at room temperature, in the presence of H2O2 solution.

The UV–vis spectra and the fluorescence intensity were recorded using a spectrophotometer (Synergy H1, BioTek, USA) (Fig. 2B. The sizes and shapes of Au NPs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy(TEM) (TECAN, Japan).

In this study, the calculation of limit of detection (LOD) was performed following the standard deviation method that is, three times of the standard deviation of blank absorbance (n = 10) divided by the slope of the regression equation.
LOD = (3 × SD)/m
where SD denotes the standard deviation of the mean blank signal and m represents the slope of the linear calibration curve.
3.1 Mechanism of SCN− detection

The sensing mechanism of SCN− is schematically presented in Fig. 1
. There are two key characteristic features of the SCN− group that play vital roles in this procedure. The first one is the hydroxyl radical (OH) scavenging ability of SCN−, which will boost the nanozymatic reaction taking place on the surface of the Au NPs (St Denis et al., 2013). Second, the ability of the SCN− group to generate ultra-small Au NPs which is well-known for its improved nanozymatic activity compared to its bulk counterpart (Ahmed et al., 2021c). Here, the synthesized GQDs act as a producing and stabilizing agent of Au NPs from HAuCl4. In the absence of the SCN− group, the size of synthesized Au NPs would be larger due to the attachment of few citrate groups on the surface of GQDs that acts as a weak reducing agent. However, the size of Au NPs becomes smaller in the presence of the SCN− group that attaches to the surface and stabilizes it. Next, the addition of the H2O2 and TMB enables the development of the characteristic, blue-colored solution caused by nanozymatic reaction. At this stage, the intensity of the color would be proportionally correlated with the concentration of SCN−, thus permitting the quantification of the SCN− group in the solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic and microscopic studies of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbance spectra of synthesized GQDs appeared at 255 nm and approximately 320 nm representing the π → π* transition of CC bond and n → π* transition of the surface states, respectively (Fig. 2B (Kang et al., 2020). The fluorescence spectroscopy study of GQDs is presented in Fig. 2C, revealing that the excitation and emission peak of GQDs was located at 340 nm and 450 nm, respectively. The microscopic study of the TEM images of the as-prepared GQDs revealed that the particles were well-dispersed in the aqueous solution, and the average size was 3–5 nm (Fig. 2D. This substantial dispersion of GQDs helps to emit the maximum wavelength and aids the enhancement of the sensitivity of analyte detection. The HR-TEM image of the GQDs showed lattice spacing of 0.34 nm that equates to the (006) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon (JCPDS 26–1076) (Fig. 2D, inset) (Kumawat, Thakur, Gurung, & Srivastava, 2017). Under UV light irradiation to GQDs, a clear blue-colored solution appeared (Fig. 2D, inset). Fluorescence lifetimes of GQDs were recorded at 450 nm using a 370 nm diode laser as the source of the excitation wavelength. The average lifetime was 1.9 ns. The calculated fluorescence quantum yield and the production yield of GQDs were 9.6 and 63% respectively.The Raman spectrum of the GQDs was presented in Fig. 2E. Two distinguished peaks appeared at 1360 and 1580 cm−1 corresponding to the D and G band, respectively. Here, the D and G bands arose due to the disorder of the sp2 hybridized carbon and the first-order scattering of the stretching vibration mode E2g for sp2 carbon, respectively. Moreover, two broad and weak bands appeared at 2700 and 2900 cm−1 conforming to the 2D and D + G bands, respectively. Those bands appeared due to the relaxation in the selection rules caused by phonon scattering at boundaries and defects in the GQDs (F. Liu et al., 2015). The XRD analysis of the GQDs revealed a broad and weak diffraction peak at 2θ = 26°, indicating the interlayer distance of 0.34 nm (Fig. S1 (Zhang et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic and microscopic studies of Au NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of thiocyanate capped Au NPs are displayed in Fig. 3 A. The NPs was spherical in shape, and their size was around 20 nm. The surface of Au NPs nanohybrids was clear and smooth. The absorbance of Au NPs investigated using UV–visible spectroscopy revealed that a sharp and single peak appeared at 550 nm (Fig. 3B, and the concentration was 3.1 nM. The smoothy surface of Au NPs was further proven through the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) images which express the presence of simple (111) and (200) crystal faces on it (Fig. 3C

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic and microscopic studies of GQDs-Au NPs hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of the GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids are presented in Fig. 3D. Compared to bare Au NPs, the surface of the nanohybrids appeared rough, most probably, due to the attachment of GQDs on the surface of Au NPs. The size and shapes of the nanohybrids were approximately 30 nm and spherical, respectively. Moreover, they were nicely dispersed in the aqueous solution. The UV–visible results of the GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids revealed that two distinguished peaks appeared at 255 nm and 600 nm corresponding to the presence of GQDs and Au NPs, respectively (Fig. 3E, and the concentration was 3.3 nM. The size of the GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids increased, and the peak shifted to the red region (600 nm) compared to bare Au NPs (550 nm), indicating the weak reducing ability of GQDs, most probably, due to the attachment of few citrate groups on its surface. The SAED images of the nanohybrids also showed the presence of different crystal faces on the surface (Fig. 3F.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic and microscopic studies of GQDs-Au NPs hybrids in the presence of SCN−
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of the GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids in the presence of SCN− revealed that the size of nanohybrids decreased (25 nm) due to the inhibitory effects of SCN− (Fig. 3G. Moreover, the presence of the SCN− group lowers the absorbance intensity of both GQDS and Au NPs and blue shifting of the characteristic peak of Au NPs (580 nm), reinforcing that SCN− group acts as a stabilizer that prevents the growth of Au NPs (Fig. 3H. However, the absorbance peak position of GQDs did not shift, revealing that SCN− group has strong interaction and effects on the growth of the Au NPs rather than the GQDs. Moreover, the effect of SCN− on the emission properties of GQDs was examined, revealing that SCN− has no effect on the GQDs fluorescence properties (Fig. S2. Hence, both inhibition and etching effects of SCN− on the AuNPs reveal a new method to quantitatively detect SCN− by monitoring the shift of the Au NPs absorption peaks. The SAED images of the nanohybrids in the presence of SCN− showed similar crystal faces of the GQDs-Au NPs hybrids only (Fig. 3I.

Furthermore, the influence of SCN− on the absorbance peak of Au NPs in the GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids was investigated by adding different concentrations of SCN−. As shown in Fig. S4, the absorbance peak of Au NPs in nanohybrids had minimally changed with the addition of varying concentrations of SCN− solution. The higher the concentration of SCN−, the lower the peak intensity and blue-shifting of absorbance peak. These results indicate that SCN− inhibits the growth of Au NPs, thus lowering the peak intensity. The blue-shifting of absorbance peaks is due to the particle size diminishing in the presence of SCN−, Here, the changes of absorbance properties depend only on the size of GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids; therefore, the changes are not significantly different. Hence, the development of SCN− sensor by monitoring the plasmonic spectra is not a suitable way to get the highest sensitivity of the detection system. Before moving forward of nanozymatic detection of SCN−, the optimization of GQDs concentration and HAuCl4 concentration is vitally crucial for the sensitivity of the proposed nanozymatic detection of SCN−. As shown in Fig. S4, the suitable concentration of GQDs and HAuCl4 for nanozymatic sensing of SCN− was 0.05 mg mL−1 and 0.05 mM, respectively. Furthermore, the concentration of H2O2 and TMB is also essential to optimization. As shown in Fig. S5, the optimized condition for H2O2 and TMB for SCN− detection was 10 mM and 5 mM, respectively. Then, the addition of TMB and H2O2 in this solution GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids makes a clearly differentiable peak in low concentrations of SCN−. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the nanozymatic peaks that appeared at 650 nm at different concentrations of the SCN− solution are well spaced out. A calibration curve of absorbance versus concentration of SCN− was constructed and showed a clear detectable peak of SCN− at the range of 10–100 nM with LOD of 3 nM (Fig. 4B. Here, two factors have boosted the color of the nanozymatic reaction as well as the sensitivity of the detection system. Firstly, the smaller the size of the GQDs-Au NPs hybrids, the higher the nanozymatic activity. In Fig. 4A, the absorbance intensity proportionally increased with the concentration of SCN−. In this case, the higher concentrated SCN− solution strongly inhibited the growth of Au NPs and smaller-sized GQDs-Au NPs hybrids showed stronger nanozymatic activity due to an increase in surface-to-volume ratio.

Secondly, the OH scavenging activity of SCN− also plays a vital role in enhancing nanozymatic activity(St Denis et al., 2013). It is a well-known mechanism that the nanozymatic activity of nanomaterials in the presence of TMB and H2O2 start with the decomposition of H2O2 to OH on the surface of nanomaterials (Fig. S6. The SCN− group that attaches on the surface of nanomaterials has strong OH scavenging activity at pH 7.5(Nishioka, Kimura, & Takahama, 1996). Hence, many OH radicals will adsorb on the surface of Au NPs (i). Ultimately, the large number of radicals on the surface of Au NPs will facilitate the oxidation reaction of TMB happening on the surface of Au NPs. Here, the Au NPs acts as a catalyst of the oxidation reaction (ii). Finally, the SCN− group assisted catalyst oxidation of TMB on Au NPs surface will produce a deep, blue-coloured solution, and the intensity of the color will be proportional to the SCN− concentration (iii). The scavenging of OH radicals on the surface of Au NPs by SCN− will bring in a new era of research in the field of nanozyme-based biosensors.

In addition, a comparison study on nanozymatic activity of similar sized SCN− capped Au NPs (∼20 nm) and SCN− capped Gr-Au NPs hybrids (∼20 nm) was performed. As shown in Fig. S7, the absorbance intensity appeared at 650 for SCN− capped Gr-Au NPs hybrids was higher than SCN− capped Au NPs. Herein, the presence of Gr QDs in SCN− capped Gr-Au NPs hybrids has boosted the nanozymatic activity. Furthermore, TA test was performed to confirm the formation of OH radicals during the reaction of SCN−-Gr-Au NPs nanohybrids. It is well-established that a fluorescence signal (fluorescent product of 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid) appears when OH radicals form and react with TA. The fluorescence spectra of 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid appear with a peak position located at 428 nm due to the formation of OH radicals (Fig, S8A). Moreover, the signal intensity of fluorescence spectra was higher for SCN−-capped GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids compared to citrate-capped GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids and SCN−-Au NPs; a good indication that SCN−-capped GQDs-Au NPs hybrids has stronger ability to form OH radicals. In addition, an experiment was performed to check the nanozymatic activity of SCN−-capped GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids in the presence of other antioxidants, for example, ascorbic acid and citric acid. As shown in Fig. S8B, the nanozymatic performance of SCN−-capped GQDs-Au NPs nanohybrids was decreased by 52.1% and 47.8% in the presence of ascorbic acid and citric acid, respectively, indicating the scavenging of some OH radicals by the antioxidants.

The selectivity of the proposed method was evaluated in the presence of some interfering ions and amino acids in raw milk samples. As shown in Fig. 5 , the absorbance of nanozymatic color developed by SCN− is much higher than the other substances indicating that the proposed method is highly selective toward SCN− over others ion and amino acids. Even the presence of thiol-containing molecules, for example, cysteine and glutathione didn’t show any interference in the present study. Most probably, the weak reducing ability and low OH radical scavenging activity of cysteine and glutathione was the reason of insignificant interference in this study. Hence, the proposed method would be a promising assay to detect SCN− in practical situations.

The practical application of the proposed SCN− was demonstrated in the spiked milk samples. As shown in Fig. 5B, the absorbance at 650 nm was linearly correlated with the concentrations of SCN− up to 10 nM with LOD of 8 nM. The absorbance intensity of the SCN− detection in milk samples was lower compared to PBS media, most probably, due to the presence of different substances that act as optical hindering for the detector. Moreover, the performance of the proposed detection assay was validated by evaluating the recovery of SCN− in commercial milk samples at various dilutions (20, 40, 60, 80 & 100 nM). The proposed assay showed 93.8–104.8% SCN− recovery with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 4% (Table S1. These results confirm the reliability and high accuracy of the proposed strategy for sensitive, specific, and convenient detection of SCN−.

A comparison study of the present work with various other reported of SCN− detection was conducted to observe its analytical performance (Table 1 . It is evident that the LOD obtained using the proposed method in this study is lower than the most of other methods except FRET based SCN− detection (Song et al., 2015). However, considering real-life applicability, colorimetric method is preferable due to the ease of operation, lower cost, and instrument free detection, and therefore, the proposed method in this article has superior advantages compared to other techniques.

An unconventional nanozymatic SCN− group detection strategy based on OH radical scavenging activity and sulfur–gold surface interaction has been presented in this study. These properties of the SCN− group could lead to changes in Au NPs size, absorption peak and can significantly alter nanozymatic activity. By exploring the change of the characteristic blue color developed by nanozymatic reactions of Au NPs in the presence of TMB and H2O2, a colorimetric quantification method for SCN− has been developed. This study showed high sensitivity with a LOD value of 3 nM. Moreover, the proposed method was successfully utilized to detect SCN− group in milk samples with LODs of 8 nM. The unique properties of SCN− that were explored in this article and found to contribute to elevated levels of nanozymatic activity will usher in a new method for surface ligand-based nanozymatic studies and will provide many promising applications and advancements in the biosensing and bioimaging fields.

Syed Rahin Ahmed: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Masoomeh Sherazee: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Seshasai Srinivasan: Supervision, Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Amin Reza Rajabzadeh: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the support from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.132152.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
